lead: Fragile items are the ones that keep people awake the night before a move, and rightly so, because they're usually the things that can't be replaced. Glassware, china, mirrors, framed photographs and that one vase from your grandmother all need more than a quick wrap and a hopeful shove into a box. Our Westerfield crews have carried delicate cargo across Suffolk since 2005, and here we share exactly how we pack fragile things so they arrive in one piece.

sections.body:
Packing fragile items safely starts well before you touch a single glass. Lay out everything you'll need so you're not scrabbling for tape with a teacup in one hand. We always work with plenty of clean packing paper, a roll or two of bubble wrap, strong double-walled boxes, good tape and a marker pen. Newspaper will do at a pinch, but the ink can transfer onto pale china, so plain paper is kinder to your good crockery.

Choose smaller, sturdier boxes for anything heavy or breakable rather than large ones. A big box tempts you to overfill it, making it heavy, awkward and far more likely to be dropped or to split at the base. Double-walled boxes give that extra rigidity that resists crushing when other items are stacked above in the van.

Before you fill anything, build a soft nest in the bottom of the box with a generous layer of scrunched paper or bubble wrap. This cushion absorbs the small knocks and vibrations of a journey along Suffolk's lanes, and it's the foundation everything else sits on. Skimping here is the single most common reason a carefully wrapped item still arrives chipped, so be generous with that base layer.

The cardinal rule with anything breakable is that no two hard surfaces should ever touch inside a box. Each glass, plate, bowl and ornament gets wrapped on its own, completely, with no bare edges peeping out. It feels slow at first, but it's the habit that saves the heartbreak of opening a box to find two favourite glasses fused together by a crack.

For glasses and stemware, gently push a twist of paper inside the bowl first, then roll the whole thing in paper or bubble wrap, paying special attention to the delicate stem. For cups with handles, wrap an extra pad around the handle, as that's the weakest point and the first thing to snap. Build up the layers until the piece feels well padded and the shape is rounded out rather than sharp.

Secure each wrap with a small piece of tape so it doesn't unravel as you pack. Hollow items like jugs and vases should be filled loosely with paper before wrapping, which stops the walls flexing and cracking under pressure. Take your time with anything genuinely precious; a few extra seconds of wrapping is far cheaper than the thing it protects.

sections.h: Pack Plates, Glasses and Bowls the Clever Way
sections.body:
How you arrange wrapped items inside the box matters as much as the wrapping itself. Plates are the classic example: counterintuitive though it sounds, they travel far better standing on their edges, like records in a rack, than stacked flat. On their edges they resist the downward pressure of a packed van; lying flat, the bottom plate takes the full weight and is the first to crack.

Wrap each plate, stand them upright in a box that's been lined with plenty of padding, and pack soft, crumpled paper firmly down the sides so nothing can shift. Glasses and stemware go upright too, ideally in a box with cardboard dividers that keep each one in its own cell. If you don't have dividers, wrap each glass thickly and pack them snugly with paper between every one.

Heavier items belong at the bottom of the box and lighter ones on top, never the other way round. Once a box is filled, fill any remaining gaps with more paper until nothing moves when you give it a gentle shake. That stillness is the test we use ourselves; if you can hear or feel things sliding, the box needs more padding before the lid goes on.

Flat, framed items like mirrors and pictures need their own approach, because a box rarely fits them and laying them flat is asking for trouble. Start by running a cross of masking tape over the glass itself. This won't stop the glass breaking, but if it does, the tape holds the shards together so they can't slice through the wrapping or scatter dangerously.

Wrap the whole frame in bubble wrap, padding the corners especially well since those are what take the impact if it's set down hard. Specialist picture boxes, which are flat and adjustable, are ideal, but a well-taped layer of cardboard on each face works too. Whatever you use, the rule is that mirrors and pictures always travel upright, on their edge, never flat where a knock from above can crack them clean across.

Lamps, vases and other odd shapes need a tailored touch. Remove lampshades and bulbs and pack them separately, wrap the base well, and stand it in a snug box padded all round. For anything tall or top-heavy, the aim is to stop it tipping or rolling, so wedge it firmly with cushions or rolled towels. Tell our crew which boxes hold these awkward items so we can load them with the care they need.

sections.h: Electronics, Lamps and Other Delicate Kit
sections.body:
Fragile doesn't only mean glass and china; plenty of everyday kit is just as easily damaged and asks for its own approach. Televisions, computers and audio equipment travel best in their original boxes if you've kept them, since those are shaped to hold each component snugly. If the boxes are long gone, wrap screens in a soft blanket first, then bubble wrap, and stand them upright rather than flat, never resting weight on the screen itself.

Before you pack any electronics, take a quick photo of how the cables connect at the back, then bag and label the leads so setting up at the other end isn't a puzzle. Remove any discs, ink cartridges or batteries that could leak or rattle loose in transit. A little preparation here saves a great deal of head-scratching when you're trying to get the television working on your first evening in the new home.

Lamps want their shades and bulbs removed and packed separately, as the shades crush easily and the bulbs shatter. Wrap the lamp base well and stand it snugly in a padded box so it can't tip or roll. The same gentle logic applies to anything with a delicate mechanism or a fragile finish; pad it, immobilise it, and keep it the right way up. Treat your kit as carefully as your crystal and it will arrive ready to use.

sections.h: Label, Load and Let Us Know
sections.body:
A perfectly packed fragile box is only as safe as the handling it gets, and that starts with clear labelling. Mark every box of breakables boldly with the word FRAGILE on more than one side, plus an arrow showing which way is up. A box labelled on a single face will inevitably end up with that face hidden, so spread the warning around where it can actually be seen.

Note the destination room too, and a quick word on the contents. When our crews unload, a clearly marked fragile box goes to the right room gently and is set down rather than slid, and you'll know not to stack anything heavy on top while you unpack at your own pace. Keeping a little inventory of your fragile boxes also helps you check everything has arrived before the van leaves.

Do tell us in advance if you're moving a lot of delicate or valuable items, or anything genuinely irreplaceable like antiques or heirloom china. We'll bring the right materials, pad the van properly and load fragile boxes where they won't be crushed. Your belongings are fully covered while they're in our hands, but a quiet word about the precious things means we treat them with the extra attention they deserve from kerb to door.

faqs: What's the best way to pack drinking glasses?

Push a twist of paper inside each glass, then wrap it in paper or bubble wrap, padding the stem of any stemware. Stand glasses upright in a divided box, or wrap them thickly and pack snugly with paper between each one. A well-padded base layer and no gaps are what stop them chipping.
Why pack plates on their edges instead of flat?

Plates resist pressure far better standing upright on their edges than stacked flat, where the bottom plate takes all the weight and cracks first. Wrap each plate, stand them like records in a padded box, and fill the gaps with crumpled paper so nothing shifts in transit.
How do I move a mirror or framed picture safely?

Run a cross of masking tape over the glass to hold any breakage together, wrap the frame and corners well in bubble wrap, and use a flat picture box if you have one. Always carry and store mirrors and pictures upright on their edge, never lying flat where a knock from above can crack them.
Should I tell the movers which boxes are fragile?

Yes. Mark every fragile box clearly on more than one side with an up arrow, and let our crew know in advance about delicate or valuable items. We'll bring the right materials, load fragile boxes where they won't be crushed, and your belongings are fully covered while we handle them.
